title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: Review - Week 15 Friday
text:
Today we review the full sequence of course projects and connect methods, interpretation, and communication.




By the end of class, you should be able to:

Summarize the research question and design for each project.
Identify what each project can/cannot claim (generalization and causation).
Explain the core method used in each project and why it fit the data type.
Interpret key outputs (estimates, p-values, intervals, model fit).
Reflect on one major strength and one limitation for each project.





Use this same checklist for Projects 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5:

Question: What was the main statistical question?
Design: SRS/convenience? RCT/observational?
Variables: Response and explanatory variable types.
Method: Plot/test/model used.
Result: Main numerical/graphical finding.
Inference: What claim is justified?
Limitations: Bias, confounding, missingness, assumptions.
Communication: One sentence rewrite in clear, ethical language.








Spend 8-10 minutes per project using the template.
Assign one speaker and one recorder per group.





Each group presents one project highlight:

best methodological choice,
biggest interpretation pitfall,
one improved conclusion sentence.






Write a brief final reflection:

Which project best demonstrates your statistical growth?
Which concept still needs review before final assessments?








Overgeneralizing from convenience samples.
Claiming causation from observational associations.
Ignoring missingness/dropout and potential bias.
Reporting p-values without checking assumptions.
Confusing statistical significance with practical importance.





Submit a one-page project synthesis:

5 bullets (one per project) with question, method, and conclusion.
1 paragraph on how your inference language improved across the term.
1 action item for strengthening your final Project 5 report.
